back_translation: Jesus called the twelve disciples to come together, then gave to them power and authority over every demon and to heal diverse diseases.
Then He sent them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick.
He commanded them saying, "Do not carry anything with you on the journey — neither staff, nor bag, nor food, nor money; and you need not have a spare outer garment either."
Whenever you enter any house, stay there until you depart from that town.
And in any town that does not welcome you, when you depart that town, shake the dust from your feet, as a testimony against them."
They then went forth preaching the good news and healing diseases throughout the villages, everywhere.
Now Herod the tetrarch-ruler of the region heard of everything that was happening, and felt exceedingly perplexed, because some were saying that John had risen from the dead.
Some said that Elijah had appeared, and others said that one of the prophets of ancient times had risen again.
Herod said, "As for John, I have already beheaded him — but who then is this one, about whom I hear such things?" And he kept striving to find opportunity to meet Jesus.
When the apostles returned, they reported to Him everything they had done. Then He took them away privately, and departed to a town called Bethsaida.
But the crowd, having learned of it, followed after Him. He welcomed them and spoke, teaching them the matter of the kingdom of God, and healed those who needed healing to recovery.

When it was near evening, the twelve disciples came and said to Him, "Please send the crowd away, so that they may go to the villages and countryside around here to find lodging and provisions, because here we are in a desolate place."
But He said to them, "You yourselves give them food to eat." They replied, "We have only five loaves of bread and two fish — unless we go buy food to feed all these people."
(There were about five thousand men.) He told His disciples, "Have them sit down in groups, about fifty per group."
They did accordingly, and made everyone sit down.
Then He took the five loaves of bread and the two fish, lifted His face up to look toward heaven, gave thanks, broke them, and handed them to the disciples to set out for distribution to the crowd.
Everyone had eaten and were satisfied, and they gathered up the leftover scraps, getting twelve baskets full.
Once, while He was praying in private, the disciples were with Him, and He asked them, "Who do the crowds say that I am?"
They replied, "Some say You are John the Baptist; some say You are Elijah; and some say You are one of the prophets from ancient times who has come back to life."
He said to them, "But as for yourselves, who do you say I am?" Peter replied, "You are the Christ of God."
He strictly warned them not to tell this to anyone at all.
He said, "The Son of Man must suffer many things, and will be rejected by the elders, the chief priests, and the scribes; He must be killed, then on the third day He will rise again anew."
Then He said to them all, "Whoever desires to follow Me, let that one deny himself, take up his cross every day, and follow Me.
For whoever desires to save his life will have to lose his life; but whoever willingly loses his life for My sake will save his life.
What profit is there, then, if a person gains the whole world as his own, but has to lose or perish from his own self?
For whoever is ashamed of Me and of My words, the Son of Man will be ashamed of that person at the time He comes in His own glory, and in that of the Father, and of the holy angels.
But I tell you all truly: among those standing here, some will not yet taste death until they have seen the kingdom of God."
About eight days after Jesus spoke these words, He took Peter, John, and James up to a mountain to pray.
While He was praying, His face was changed, and His garments became white and shone radiantly like lightning.
Suddenly there were two men conversing with Him — namely, Moses and Elijah.
Both appeared in glory, speaking of His departure, which He was about to accomplish in Jerusalem.
As for Peter and his companions, all were heavy with sleep such that their eyes would close; but when they were awake, they saw His glory and the two men who were standing with Him.

As the two men were about to depart from Him, Peter said to Jesus, "Master, it is good for us to be here; let us build three shelters — one for You, one for Moses, and one for Elijah." (He did not understand what he was saying.)
While Peter was still speaking, a cloud came and overshadowed them, and they were afraid when they entered into the cloud.
And a voice came from the cloud, saying, "This is My Son, the one I have chosen. Listen to Him."
When the voice had finished speaking, Jesus alone was found. The disciples kept silent, and at that time did not tell anyone about the things they had seen.
The next day, when they came down from the mountain, a large crowd came to meet Him.
Behold, a man in the crowd cried out, "Teacher sir, I beg You to please look at my son, because he is my only child.
Behold, a spirit has possessed him, and suddenly he screams and convulses violently such that foam comes out at his mouth; the spirit hardly ever departs from him, and injures him severely.
I pleaded with Your disciples to cast it out, but they could not do it."
Jesus replied, "O generation of this age — how unbelieving and perverse! How long must I be with you and put up with you? Bring your son here."
While the boy was coming, the demon threw him down, causing him to convulse violently. But Jesus rebuked that unclean spirit, healed the boy to recovery, and returned him to his father.
Everyone was amazed at the great might of God. While everyone was marveling at all the things He was doing, He said to His disciples,
"Set these words into your ears carefully, because the Son of Man will have to be handed over into human hands."
But they did not understand this saying, because it had been hidden from them so that they could not perceive it, and they were afraid to ask Him about that saying.
Then an argument arose among the disciples about who among them would be the greatest.
But Jesus, knowing the thoughts in their hearts, led a little child to stand beside Himself,

and He said to them, "Whoever welcomes such a little child in My name welcomes Me; and whoever welcomes Me welcomes the One who sent Me. Because whoever is the lowest among you all — that one is the great one."
John replied, "Master, we saw a man casting out demons in Your name, so we forbade him, because he does not follow You together with us."
Jesus replied to him, "Do not forbid him, because whoever is not opposed to you is on your side."
When the day drew near for Him to be taken up, He set His heart firmly on going to Jerusalem,
and He sent messengers ahead of Him. They went to a village of the Samaritans to prepare a welcome for Him.
But the people of that town refused to welcome Him, because He was headed toward Jerusalem.
When His disciples — James and John — saw the event, they said, "Lord, do You wish us to command fire to come down from heaven and destroy them utterly?"
But He turned and rebuked them.
Then He and the disciples went on to another village.
As they were traveling along, a certain person said to Him, "I will follow You wherever You go."
Jesus replied to him, "Foxes still have dens, and birds of the air have nests; but the Son of Man has no place to lay down His head."
He said to another, "Follow Me." But that one replied, "Lord, please permit me to go bury my father first."
He said to him, "Let the dead bury their own dead; as for you, go proclaim the kingdom of God."
Still another one said, "Lord, I will follow You, but please let me first go bid farewell to those who are in my house."
Jesus said, "No one who takes hold of a plow and then turns to look back is fit for the kingdom of God."
